speaker: interviewer
text: February the 15th, 1995. Jack Pinto, the survivor. Interviewer, Iris Lasky. Woodland Hills, Los Angeles, USA. And it will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: --cinematography, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can go. It's OK-- it's running.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Jack Pinto. J-A-C-K, P-I-N-T-O. Born August 18, 1907, in the city of Amsterdam, country of Holland. I am the interviewer, Iris Lasky. We are in the city of Woodland Hills, state of California, in the United States of America. And today's date is Wednesday, February 15, 1995. Jack, would you please begin by stating your full name and age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name when I was born was Jacob Jessurun Pinto. So long we stayed in Europe, I could hold that name. But when we came to America, we're not allowed to have two names. So they changed it to Jack Pinto. I went-- my parents went to England when I was three months old. In tobacco business. He worked for the Imperial Tobacco Company in London. And we lived there nearly 18 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember when the First World War broke out. That was in 1914. When the Germans started the war in England, you know? And what happened-- my father was still Dutch. Didn't have to go into service, but he had to work by the farmers in the field, digging to help the food for the British people with his two brothers, who were also in England. They came after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we lived there-- went to school-- getting bigger. We lived there about 18 years. And my upbringing was in the British schools-- in Stoke Newington. I remember that. It's many years ago. It's very hard to remember all those things. We had a happy life-- happy family. And my brother who was younger me and two sisters who were younger me were born in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? Or what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Hyman. My sister's name is Katy. And the other one name is Jinny. And they all still are three living.

speaker: interviewer
text: You described your family as a happy family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some of the things that you did as a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father let me learn piano in that time. I remember my sister learning the violin. And we were always learning good in school. I went to a-- and then I went in some plays, like school plays-- and I remember that. And I sang many times with the school band-- the jazz, what I love. That was in the time when Paul Whiteman started. And I was looking at it. And then we grew up there. And I played some soccer, in London, as a boy. I went to school there up till my 16th year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And during the war, in 1914, I can never forget-- I was standing on the balcony-- I saw the zeppelins coming over, that time, being shot down, on fire-- see the Germans falling out. I was seven years old that time. That memory, I remember very good. And then, after that, 1914-- 1918, the war stopped. And I was 18. I remember in between, after the war stopped, we went back to Holland for my bar mitzvah to be celebrated in the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in Amsterdam. For a holiday. All the family were living in England with us, that time. Then we went back to England again. And after a few years, my father said he wants to go and live in Holland again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the Jewish community in Holland when you went back there. What was it like in prewar years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a-- I was a member of the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in London. Bevis Marks-- that was the name, also. The synagogue was just built like the one in Amsterdam, but smaller. And I was in the choir there-- in the choir of the Spanish and Port-- and then, I remember-- I don't wear it now, but I got a tzitzit and everything, because-- And then, after that, we enjoyed our-- member of the North London Jewish Club in London, where we had plays and sports and everything. I made a good friend there-- Shapiro was his name. And we stuck together always, till the time my father said, I want to go back to Holland and start a factory of my own. Then the whole family went-- after 18 years, we went back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Holland like when you came back at 18 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a very good time. After that, you know, the-- what do they call that, when it went bad in America and everything? Just before the-- what do they call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Depression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depression, yes. But it went good. We didn't have no worries. We worked, and I worked-- I start working when I was 16, by a furrier. But I didn't like it. Two years. And when we went back to Holland, when I was 18 years old-- 18, 19-- I went to learn the barber trade. And that I stuck up to, till I retired. But when I learned the barber trade, that was one of the things that helped me very much to save my life in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to do an apprenticeship to become a barber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Europe, you don't do that. Here, you have to. You work. When you start, you've got to sweep the floor, you've got to shave the people with the soap, and then they learn you how to shave and everything. And after that, learned haircutting and everything. But here is a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came here back-- no-- I wanted to go to the-- when the Russians were coming forward, going to Hungary, my present wife, then-- oh, let me tell you of my first wife-- before we came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went back to Holland. And there, in the barber shop, a little boy came in. He was about 14 years old. His father wanted him to learn the trade. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name was Ziff. Ziff-- Max Ziff.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came in one day-- and that's how it all started-- with a banjo! I said, what you doing with a banjo? He said, I can play jazz. And he started playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm looking at him. I said, you don't want to be a barber! It's not good to be a barber-- that talent. He said, I know, I play violin, I play saxophone. And his father came in the shop. I said, don't let him be that. So I was invited by him at home, and that's where I met my wife, is this. Ella. Later, he became a well-known musician. Saxophonist. And when we got married--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met Ella at that time at, at the Ziffs' home.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Ziffs' home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you met Ella, how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 25, yeah. Yeah, about 25 years old. And she was seven years younger than me, I remember that. And we began to love one another. And we were-- like, in Europe, you're engaged for a couple of years, to save your towels and all that-- you build it up. And that's what we done. And then we got married in 1936-- in September 1936. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were two years married when my first child come. That was Sientje. And after that my son was born, I think on the 20 September, 1938. No, '38-- 19-- I've got it on my-- it's on the papers what I have here. And then we got married. We had a real Sephardic wedding, because I was Sephardic. And we married in the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in Amsterdam. We were six years married-- happy. I was working as a barber. She was working in a-- like, a shop like-- not Selfridges. I mean, like the Bijenkorf, in Amsterdam. That was a big warehouse, like-- like here, the May Company. And after that, when we had the children, she stopped. And then, 1940, we were going to have a big party that Saturday. My neighbors were 12 and a half-- Jewish people-- were 12 and a half years married. In Europe, they called that the Copper Wedding. They make a big party. And we would rehearse for everything. And that same night, the Germans came bombing with the bombs. And then we didn't have the party. It was cancelled. And then it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into the war experiences, Jack, let's go back to, what was your home-- you and Ella and Sientje and Joseph together-- how did you-- how did you do your Jewish observances? What type of home did you have? Describe it. We had an apartment in Retiefstraat. That was the name where my apartment was, in the east of Amsterdam. We had a nice home, two lovely children, and a beautiful wife-- so beautiful. But they didn't like Jews that was blonde and blue-eyed, you know. And everywhere I went with them, everybody said, what beautiful children, you know? And then everything went fine till '45-- 1940. What were the relationships between the Jews and the non-Jews in Holland before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no antisem--

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no hate-- no antisemitism. Nothing at all. Nothing. Very-- of course, we have Christian friends. I went in the Social Democratic sports club, where we were swimming and everything. We went with canoes, when I was living in-- we went to the countryside and through the canals in Amsterdam. Well, that was-- when I was married, then. And after that, when the war broke out, that's when the sorrows began.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals, as a young person, when you and your wife and children-- did you have goals for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was-- I was-- had goals to sing with the bands, because I was well known, and I sang a lot of jazz and everything at that time. And I sang in clubs and also in a Jewish club and that. And then everything's changed when the war broke out. Everything changed. We were afraid. The Germans came, and then they started. In 1941, there was, like, a-- a rebel with the Christian people, because they were taking the Jews away-- that was well known in Amsterdam-- in February of 1941. They have still a statue on it, in front of the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in Amsterdam, about that rebellion. And then, that same day, I was walking, coming from my father's shop, they put the bridges up. I couldn't pass the bridges. And then the big trucks came in-- that was in February 1941-- picking everything-- the Jews away. We didn't have the badges yet. That came later. But they could see. It was like-- they called it a 'ghetto' that time. It wasn't. And then I fled. And went up in the apartment on the second floor, and I banged on the door. They opened it. There was a Jewish man and a Christian woman. They were-- Bessemer. I remember the name-- Bessemer. And there I looked behind a curtain. I see them all on their knees, every one of them, being hitting and that. And then they were put in big trucks and taken away. Once it was gone, they put the bridges down. And then we heard they went to Mauthausen. Only one came back, from the about nearly 300 of them. One-- one came back. He's in Holland. I don't know where he is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, after that, we had to work, with shovels-- in the farms, you know? They put us to work there. In the meantime-- That was in 1941, you're talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And, in the meantime, my wife got scarlet fever, when I was working, so I was allowed to go home. I was two months in that work camp. And I stayed with her. And then she got pregnant again, after we had the two children. And she was afraid, and I was afraid. I said, perhaps we can get an abortion. And we got it illegal. She went in the hospital, I remember, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible, you know-- illegal abortion, at that time. If I wouldn't have had it done, the child would have been born, it would have been in the gas chamber anyway. And then we stuck together, every time, afraid to go on the street, afraid there could-- then we had to put the stars on, you know, the-- afraid to go on the street. Every moment, they were picking people up from the street. I stayed so much as I can at home. And then I went to the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue, where I got a badge that I can come in there. And then, after that, in 1942--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on to 1942-- we'll come back-- what did you know about the rise of Nazism prior to the Germans coming in and occupying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I read about it in the paper, what was going on in Germany when they occupied the Sudet-- different countries, you know. The Studen-- the Studen-- what was the name--? Yeah. And then, after that, they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you discuss it among your family and your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were afraid to go to my parents. They'd pick us up. We had  the star on. And then we stayed at home. Ah, we're getting some-- somebody coming in. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before the Germans came to the-- to Holland, or to Amsterdam, when you were hearing these stories, what were you telling-- what were you discussing--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't think they were going to come in Holland. We didn't think about it. We didn't think-- we heard about it. You know, I remember-- as child, from the pogroms, we heard it was going on, but we didn't think about it. But now it started with us. And then we see how they were hitting the people, now this. Then we knew what was going on. Then in 1942, on the 27th of July, bang on the door. I had to get dressed. My children had to get dressed. And we were brought to the Dutch-- they call that the Holland Stadsschouwburg-- the Dutch theatre. It was about a half-hour walking from us. And there we put all the Jews in there, waiting to be transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the four of you, together?

speaker: interviewee
text: The four of us, together. And then we were brought to Westerbork, where we stayed three months, because I was still trying to get papers because my wife is British. Her father was interned. Her mother died when she was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then three months in Westerbork. And then, all of a sudden, in October 1942, I was called to get ready to go in the train. It wasn't cattle-- it was normal trains, you know? No cattle runs with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we drove there for two days, with the children sitting on my lap. And then it stopped in the place-- what's the name-- it's on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cosel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that's when they came with whips-- opened the doors. All men, [SPEAKING GERMAN]! And hitting us. Not the older ones, but the young ones. And I was pulled out. I hear my daughter-- Daddy, Daddy, Daddy! In Dutch, she was calling-- [SPEAKING DUTCH]! And my wife. And they went, and I never saw them back again. And from there, from Cosel, we went two days in a camp, there. And from there we went to the other camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember, being on that train together with your wife and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nervous-- crying. The children, you know, crying-- we didn't know what's going to happen. We didn't know, and we thought we were going to be put with the family together. That's what we thought. We didn't know nothing about gas chambers and all that thing. We thought we were going to be-- they promise we're going to be the family, together, but it wasn't true. And that's when they took me away and I never saw them back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was OK. We had food with us, what we took. That, we used to get up. They didn't give-- didn't give nothing in the two and a half days. And when the train stopped, though, that's when it started-- when they hit me with the rifle, and this. And then we had to march to a place, and I never saw them back again. [SOBS] They were so beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBS] It won't go away from me. And then we went to that one camp, where I stayed nine months. Now in this camp, it was taken over by the Polish police. Not the Nazis. And we stayed there nine months. But we had to-- They were also antisemitic. Oh, they were terrible. And then the head of the police came in, from Schoppinitz three times a week. If somebody was sick, he kicked them till they were dead. And I got the job-- because I was a barber, I could cut their hair. You had to be bald. And even the man who took care of the camp, I cut him. So I could stay in the camp, cleaning up the wards and everything. And that went on for nine months. From the kitchen I stole food. I helped some of the people, you know, where-- and that went on for nine months. And then the train passed by on a Sunday-- that was in August 1943-- shooting. And they got two people. One was shot. His name was Jacobs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one was killed. I forgot his name who was killed. And then they tried to say he had a bullet in his lung. They were trying to do anything to see if they could help. No doctors-- no doctors there. But he suffered terrible. And then another one came back from this transport. He was hit by the train near Cracow, where he was working. So when he came there. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is at Schoppinitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schoppinitz. And at Schoppinitz they put the bodies on a barrel. And I had to go-- there was another one with me-- with the Polish police behind me, and throw them in a grave. In Schoppinitz. There was a Jewish burial grounds. I threw about 20 in. Then I went back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going through your mind-- your mind when you were doing these--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing those things-- well, your, your thinking was-- when you saw-- you get used to it, those dead bodies. You get used to it. You don't know what to do. I hope it doesn't happen to me. Every time he came in, this head from the police, he kicks them until they were dying. It was terrible. If they were sick, they couldn't go to work. They were finished off. And then, after nine months, on November 1943 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we all had to come together, and we were put in a train. About 45 minutes away where we were. And that's when we went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Auschwitz, up to that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- nothing. Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the train ride to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train ride was like in a normal train. Although we had  our clothes with the badge on, and everything. And when we were there, all our clothes were taken off. And we were sitting there, freezing. It was in November. And my friend-- who's living in Montreal-- he's the only one [INAUDIBLE] me from where we were. He lives in Montreal. His name is William Pollock. But we were to-- through him I came to Canada. But then, when we were there, sitting there, nearly 24 hours, freezing and cold. And then, all of a sudden, the SS came in. You've got to go in the sauna. We had to be washed in the sauna. Well, our heads was bald already, so we didn't have to cut it. And there we stayed. They gave us a uniform-- you know, the white stripe-- the striped uniform, the stripes. And there I was-- went to Birkenau. Birkenau was-- first was Auschwitz and Birkenau, were connected to one another. That's where their gas chambers was-- in Birkenau. And there we went into quarantine. Two weeks. That was terrible. Mengele-- my story, I have a story-- Mengele came in our barracks. They say, Mengele's coming. And Mengele came in, after two weeks there. We had to all undress-- stand in a line. We were about 350. And then he started pointing. This side to the left, to the right. Only 17 of us went to the right, and I was a one who went to the right. All of them went in different barracks, screaming and crying because they knew they were finished. The next day, when I went out my barrack, it was empty. They all went to the gas chamber the next morning, every one of them. And I remember my uncle, who I met there, walking-- help me, help me. I couldn't do nothing. He was what they call a muselmann. They were so thin and everything. And then from there they transport me to-- from the quarantine, to the barracks in Birkenau. It was all Birkenau. It was a different block. I went in block 18. I remember the block. And there we had to work. Up in the morning at 5 o'clock-- in the night, they came, hitting us. If the barrel was-- you know, if people had got the bowel, we had barrels. We had to empty them, in turns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one-- they-- one night, I'm awake, with somebody with his bread in his pocket, laying dead next to me. So what do you do? You take that piece of bread. You couldn't help it. And I put it in pocket, and I laid down. You feel the rats going over you, trying to get in the-- in the-- in the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got shovels and all kind of [INAUDIBLE]. And we went on the march every morning to a place where-- it was cold-- ice-- and we had to dig the ice away and everything. And 50% were dead when we came back. They went to the gas chamber. They were dead. They were burned. But what happened-- one day, like angels, with the Alexander Ragtime Band. When you walked out of the camp, they play music. They have a band. And this band were also prisoners. There were a few Dutch in the band who I knew. One lives in Canada also. Bunette is his name. He still blows a trumpet and-- And I used to be in, in that kind of-- the Alexander Ragtime Band. I never forget it. And I'm singing-- crying and singing the words, you know. And next to me is a sold-- Nazi, standing next to me, and he's listening. And he said, go on, go on, sing, sing. I'm crying. He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], 'don't cry,' in German. So I'm wondering why. He says, I want those words.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we come to the place where we had to shovel and all that kind of stuff. And he called me, separate. He told me, how do I know that? So I told I was born up in England. I love jazz. I used to sing with bands. I said, in German-- I could speak a little German-- how come you like that? He said, I was born in the United States. My parents came from Germany. They didn't like America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Hitler occupied America-- he was young then-- he was about 14 years old, I think-- his parents went back to Germany. And he was brought up as a Nazi-- the Hitler Youth. And then he became an SS. But his memories from the-- he always loved that. He told me he plays at home sometimes. His parents didn't like it, but he done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote the words for him. He said, I'll try to get you in another kommando. Now I'm-- transports were coming in by the thousands, from Hungary, in May 1944. Thousands from Czechoslovakia. And they had to be cut-- all their hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he talked, and I was picked out to be, by the group, barbers that they had. I had to cut, I'm ashamed to say, the woman between the legs and everything-- I had to do, with the SS behind me. There was a day shift and a night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: My friend-- or still a good friend of mine-- he worked-- you ever heard of the Kanada, the Kanada? He worked for the Kanada. That was the one, when they came out the trains, they had to take the luggages and put it all away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, always, they threw their luggage where we were nearly working, near the [INAUDIBLE], in the corner. And he said, if they're not looking, go over to the luggage and see. And that's what-- we look-- we saw all what we saw, celery, walnuts, and everything in the food. And we hided it, before they could see us. And when we went out of my shift, then he got a pail, and we made soup behind the barracks that the Germans didn't see. Well, one day I was caught by one of the Nazis with a piece of bread I took from the kitchen. My whole face was blue. And I see that one soldier, who gave me the chance. He goes, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your face was blue because--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitting me, because I stole a piece of bread. But I still worked as a barber. But then they put me in the sauna, where I had to put on the showers, with the woman naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember one woman, she survived. She lives in Amsterdam. Had a gold chain which she was hiding. She said, can you get me something for that? I said, I'll try. And then the people came in from-- with barrels with potatoes and that. And they were trying to get things. And they were Polish. And I gave them the chain. They gave me a big piece of sausage-- blood sausage, you know? And I gave her that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the stuff what we got in the-- in the-- in the clothes, from the people-- we sometimes tore it open when they weren't looking. Diamonds and everything. And we hided them. My friend-- in the war, we couldn't find him anymore, after the war. They had so much gold and everything. And then, when they came out the sauna, they had to go and work-- the woman. And three months later, I saw two of the woman. They were only bones, you know. And they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a few of them went to the experiment block. You heard of the experiments, what they done? Tell us about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One woman survived.
